original_abstract: This chapter provides a comprehensive evaluation of the global water cycle, synthesizing multiple lines of evidence to track its past, present, and projected future states. It builds upon foundational findings regarding climate extremes and regional change, incorporating a wide array of data sources—including paleoclimate reconstructions, satellite observations, atmospheric reanalyses, and the latest generation of global and regional model simulations. The assessment highlights the physical drivers behind water cycle shifts, offers a formal attribution of observed changes to human influence, and explores future projections alongside their inherent uncertainties and the risk of abrupt transitions. Our findings reveal that human activities have already caused widespread and non-uniform alterations to the water cycle. While these shifts were frequently obscured during the 20th century by the competing effects of different climatic drivers, such as aerosol cooling, the global water cycle will be increasingly and more clearly dominated by greenhouse gas forcing as the 21st century progresses.
humanized_abstract: A physical basis for changes in extreme precipitation and potential evaporation. This chapter assesses the current state of knowledge of the water cycle — past, present and future — synthesizing as many of the various lines of evidence as feasible. In addition to considering climate change and changes in components of the regional climate in light of the prior chapters of this report, it draws on a broad range of information sources, including: - Paleoclimate reconstructions - Satellites - Atmospheric reanalyses - Newer global and regional climate models That part of the water cycle that occurs within the atmosphere and at the surface can be attributed to physical mechanisms that enable changes in extreme precipitation and potential evaporation to occur. Attributing changes in the global water cycle to the causes to which they have occurred is a formal activity — it makes explicit the attribution of recent changes to estimated causes and considers the implications for the water cycle of projected changes and their respective uncertainties including the possibility of abrupt changes. The human-caused changes to the water cycle have been real, pervasive and highly nonuniform. The water cycle effects of climate change and other factors during the 20th century were sometimes masked by the effects of aerosols. It will be a different climate change scenario, however, in which greenhouse gases are the dominant forcing.
